mappings.rationaleAI: Education covering aims, learning theories, teaching methods, and historical development from ancient to modern directly addresses systems of education (A) and history of education (B).
Schools as institutions varying globally across public, private, religious, and alternative models address types of educational systems and their characteristics (A.3).
The Renaissance's recovery of classical antiquity, humanism, and spread across Italy and Europe directly addresses European education during the Renaissance (B.5).
The Middle Ages' Church and monasticism, scholasticism and universities directly address education in the European Middle Ages (B.3).
The Byzantine Empire's Greek Christian and humanistic culture directly addresses Byzantine education (B.2.b).
Charlemagne's promotion of literacy and the Carolingian Renaissance directly addresses the cultural revival under Charlemagne (B.3.b).
The Enlightenment's emphasis on reason, encyclopedias, and rational reform addresses European education in the 17th and 18th centuries (B.6).
The Reformation's role in promoting literacy and the Counter-Reformation's educational response directly address education during the Reformation and Counter-Reformation (B.5.d).
The Islamic Golden Age's intensive intellectual production in Baghdad, Cordoba, and Cairo directly addresses Islamic education (B.2.d).
Ancient Rome's Latin language, literature, and educational institutions directly address ancient Roman education (B.1.e).
Ancient Greece's philosophy, literature, and intellectual traditions directly address ancient Greek education (B.1.d).
Confucius's teachings on education and moral cultivation directly address ancient Chinese education (B.1.b) and Chinese education shaped by Confucian principles (B.4.b).
Napoleon's lycee system and administrative centralization of education address development of national systems of education in the 19th century (B.7.c).
Catherine's founding of universities and the Smolny Institute for women's higher education addresses European education in the 18th century (B.6).
Gutenberg's printing press catalyzed Renaissance and Reformation educational movements (B.5) and the dissemination of knowledge in the 17th and 18th centuries (B.6).
The Holy Roman Empire's scholastic traditions and Reformation educational transformations address education in the European Middle Ages (B.3) and education during the Reformation (B.5.d).

Post-classical history (500-1500 CE) spanning Islamic Golden Age, Tang-Song China, and European feudalism connects to education in Islamic, Byzantine, and medieval European civilizations (B.2, B.3) and Asian civilizations (B.4).
The modern era's nationalism, industrialism, and political revolution address Western education in the 19th century (B.7) and education in the 20th century (B.8).
European history from classical antiquity through modern integration provides the framework for understanding the history of education across all European periods (B).
Globalization's interdependence of economies and cultures connects to modernization of education in Asia and Africa (B.8.c) and education in newly emerging nations (B.8.d).
Romanticism's emphasis on individual development and imagination connects to early reform movements in pedagogy and psychology in the 19th century (B.7.b).
